design_philosophy.core_loop: Run, jump, and collect through themed levels; defeat enemies by jumping on them, striking with tail, or throwing collected nuts; gather coins for completion bonuses
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Navigation hazards and enemy encounters balanced with exploration for hidden coins and health pickups; time pressure for bonus points optional
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as Mr. Nutz, an anthropomorphic red squirrel stopping Mr. Blizzard the yeti from freezing the world; collecting nuts as ammunition and coins as currency
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: No mandatory time limits per level; player-controlled pacing allows leisurely exploration vs speed-running for bonuses. Generous checkpoint system via passwords (in most versions) balances accessibility with progression gating. Nut collection mechanic creates resource management without scarcity pressure.

difficulty.lives_system: Player starts with limited lives (amount varies by difficulty setting and port); losing all health costs one life; resets current level
difficulty.continues_system: Game Over allows continue from stage start with default lives, health, and zero nuts/coins/score accumulated
difficulty.checkpointing: Password system in all versions except original SNES; passwords appear at certain level milestones allowing mid-stage restart. Original SNES lacks passwords, requiring full playthrough per session
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Difficulty setting affects starting lives and health units. No time pressure (optional speed bonus). Player can choose leisurely exploration or aggressive progression. Hidden health/extra lives reward thorough play.

progression.upgrade_system: Nut collection for combat ammunition; health pickups and extra life items scattered throughout levels
progression.unlock_mechanism: Six themed levels unlock sequentially; boss defeat (Mr. Blizzard at end) gates world-saving narrative. Password system unlocks mid-stage checkpoints
replayability_hooks: Coin collection for completion bonuses creates optimization incentive
Hidden coins and lives reward thorough exploration
Difficulty modes with different life/health allocations
Level select cheat code enables rapid replays and skill challenges

world_building_techniques: Six thematically distinct levels (ice, water, etc. implied by level removal/remake notes) suggest environmental worldbuilding
Character premise: anthropomorphic squirrel hero vs yeti villain establishes tone and stakes
No cutscene documentation suggests minimal cinematic delivery; story likely conveyed via level design and character interaction

boss_design.philosophy: Primary antagonist is Mr. Blizzard, the yeti attempting to freeze the world; suggests single final boss encounter rather than per-stage bosses, though documentation lacks specific mechanics

faction_indicators: Mr. Nutz (squirrel protagonist) vs Mr. Blizzard (yeti antagonist) represents nature/warmth vs cold/ice ideology
danger_signals: Yeti attempting magical world domination through ice; apocalyptic stakes (freeze the world)
Generic platformer enemy encounters suggest environmental hazards and minion opposition
environmental_storytelling: Six themed levels imply environmental variety and climatic progression toward frozen/icy final confrontation
Removed water stage and bonus levels in GBA remake suggest environmental diversity in original design
Ice/cold motif connected to villain suggests thematic level design (e.g., increasingly frozen environments)

sources_used: Wikipedia (Mr. Nutz article): gameplay mechanics, development history, character design, release dates, narrative premise
The Cutting Room Floor (Mr. Nutz SNES): developer credits, level select cheat, hidden content
